channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Losing Trust in Justice Dept., Judges Call Out Its Lawyers’ Behavior
item.description: The federal courts have long assumed that the government’s lawyers are trustworthy. Now judges across the country are criticizing their lack of candor.
item.dc:creator: Mattathias Schwartz
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: The notion that Justice Department lawyers can be taken at their word, a longtime assumption that allows federal courts to operate swiftly and smoothly, now appears to be in jeopardy.

item.title: Hegseth Strikes Female and Black Navy Officers From Promotion List
item.description: The defense secretary’s decision to block the officers’ promotions appears driven by his anti-diversity stance rather than based on merit.
item.dc:creator: Greg Jaffe and Kate Kelly
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth’s removal of at least seven officers from the list appears to violate rules governing the promotion system, according to current and former defense officials.

item.title: How Lebanon’s Best Chance to Disarm Hezbollah Failed
item.description: Lebanon’s government has long wanted the powerful militia to give up its weapons. Before the Iran war began, there were signs of progress toward that goal.
item.dc:creator: Abdi Latif Dahir and Aaron Boxerman
item.category: Arms Control and Limitation and Disarmament
item.media:description: Climbing through rubble in central Beirut, Lebanon, after an Israeli airstrike in March.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump as Middle East Tensions Build
item.description: Some analysts said the main international oil price, which was up 6 percent on Monday, could climb much higher in the coming weeks if the Strait of Hormuz doesn’t reopen.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca F. Elliott and Joe Rennison
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:description: An oil refinery in Carson, Calif. The possibility of continued fighting has raised concerns about how long the world’s stockpiles of oil and fuels will last if the Strait of Hormuz does not reopen.

item.title: Anthropic Files to Go Public, Setting Stage for Huge I.P.O.
item.description: The artificial intelligence company, which is racing OpenAI to the stock market, has seen explosive growth over the last year thanks largely to technology that can automatically write computer code.
item.dc:creator: Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Jason Henry for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anthropic is one of the high-profile tech companies preparing to go public this year.

item.title: California’s Public Universities Went All in on A.I. Now They’re Tearing Themselves Apart.
item.description: California’s public universities spent $16.9 million on A.I. during a financial crisis, and the result has been chaos.
item.dc:creator: Linda Kinstler

item.title: How Box Created 13 New Types of Jobs Because of A.I.
item.description: Box, a Silicon Valley software maker, expects to have more employees, not fewer, as it hires A.I. architects, A.I. solutions managers and other new A.I.-related positions.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang
item.media:credit: Peter Prato for The New York Times
item.media:description: “We ourselves are selling A.I. to our customers, so that’s actually causing us to need to hire more people,” said Box’s chief executive, Aaron Levie.

item.title: Is A.I. Replacing Tech Workers or Providing an Excuse for Job Cuts?
item.description: Tech industry layoffs are accelerating, and executives have been quick to say it’s because their companies are doing more with artificial intelligence, even when there may be more to it.
item.dc:creator: Kalley Huang

item.title: Scott Pelley Accuses CBS News Boss of ‘Murdering’ ‘60 Minutes’
item.description: In an explosive staff meeting, Mr. Pelley, a correspondent for the long-running Sunday news show, blasted Bari Weiss, the CBS editor in chief, and Nick Bilton, the show’s new executive producer.
item.dc:creator: Michael M. Grynbaum and Benjamin Mullin

item.title: The Return of Blaming and Shaming in Public Health
item.description: For years, medicine has tried to eliminate stigma. Now, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. is bringing back the language of personal responsibility.
item.dc:creator: Simar Bajaj
item.category: Centers for Disease Control and Prevention
item.media:credit: Claire Merchlinsky/The New York Times; Photographs by Getty

item.title: Are Texans Ready for Talarico’s Kind of Christianity?
item.description: Jim Rigby, a pastor who rarely uses the word “God,” is a key to understanding the Senate candidate trying to pull off something unusual in Texas.
item.dc:creator: Ruth Graham and J. David Goodman
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jim Rigby has led St. Andrew’s Presbyterian Church in Austin, Texas, since the 1980s.

item.title: They Voted for Trump. Here’s How They Feel About High Gas Prices.
item.description: With the midterm elections five months away, many Americans who backed President Trump are wrestling with pain at the pump and the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: Tim Balk, Rachel Richardson and Sam Easter
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)

item.title: Alberta’s Movement to Separate from Canada Gets Its Moment
item.description: In October, Albertans will get to say if they want to stay in Canada, or hold a referendum to leave. Will it settle the matter, or deepen the rift?
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff and Amber Bracken
item.media:description: A table set up to gather signatures for a separation petition along a road in Calgary, Alberta, in February.

item.title: Alberta Will Vote on a Possible Referendum to Separate From Canada. Here’s What to Know.
item.description: Premier Danielle Smith announced that she would ask citizens to vote on whether they want to stay in Canada, or hold a referendum to secede.
item.dc:creator: Matina Stevis-Gridneff
item.media:description: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith at a news conference in Calgary on May 22, 2026.

item.title: Joana Avillez Revisits Her Waterfront Childhood
item.description: Joana Avillez took six years to illustrate a new edition of Joseph Mitchell’s “The Bottom of the Harbor,” which captures the salty New York neighborhood of her youth.
item.dc:creator: Alex Vadukul
item.media:credit: Elias Williams for The New York Times
item.media:description: Joana Avillez poses for a portrait not far from the building where she grew up, near the South Street Seaport in Lower Manhattan.

item.title: Inside the NYC Primary Between Antonio Reynoso and Claire Valdez
item.description: The Democratic battle over a House seat in New York City has reignited old tensions over gentrification, identity and who gets to define the left.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Adam Gray for The New York Times
item.media:description: Mr. Reynoso has received the backing of major labor unions.

item.title: What to Watch For in the NY Primary: Key Races and Why They Matter
item.description: Congressional primaries in Manhattan, Brooklyn and beyond could test the strength of Mayor Zohran Mamdani’s socialist movement and reverberate across the country.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Fandos
item.media:credit: Victor J. Blue for The New York Times
item.media:description: Competitive primary contests are taking place in six House districts in New York City and elsewhere in the state.

item.title: China Aims A.I. at Predicting Who Could Pose a Political Risk
item.description: New research examines how a Chinese company struggled to develop its predictive surveillance technology while U.S. restrictions were in place.
item.dc:creator: Julian E. Barnes
item.media:credit: Lam Yik Fei for The New York Times
item.media:description: The skyline of Beijing. A Chinese firm is working on products that use A.I. to examine location data and internet use to predict who could do or say something critical of the government.

item.title: Chinese Military Sought Nvidia Chips for Years, Report Says
item.description: An analysis of six years of procurement records suggests that the People’s Liberation Army has openly tried to acquire restricted U.S. technology.
item.dc:creator: Ana Swanson and Tripp Mickle
item.media:description: China’s military has stepped up its efforts to acquire artificial intelligence chips despite U.S. restrictions, according to Wirescreen, a software platform.

item.title: Sam Levinson on Why ‘Euphoria’ Had to End This Way. For Good.
item.description: In an exclusive interview, Sam Levinson explained why he felt it was time to bring the series to a close and responded to its detractors.
item.dc:creator: Joe Coscarelli, Jon Caramanica and Caroline Tompkins
item.media:credit: Caroline Tompkins for The New York Times

item.title: In the ‘Euphoria’ Finale, the HBO Show Goes Out With a Whimper
item.description: In the end, a show defined by its excesses seemed to be straining for a redemption that it didn’t need.
item.dc:creator: Mike Hale
item.media:description: Rue (Zendaya) has been the guide and moral center of “Euphoria” from the beginning.

item.title: Marco Rubio: Florida Centrist, Tea Party Darling, MAGA Warrior, President?
item.description: The secret to the Secretary of State’s success.
item.dc:creator: Manuel Roig-Franzia

item.title: Why This Summer Travel Season Will Be a Nightmare
item.description: Air travel could be disrupted into next year because of high jet fuel prices.
item.dc:creator: R. Michael Baiada and Robert W. Mann Jr.

item.title: Giant Stone Urns Hint at the Death Rites of a Lost People in Laos
item.description: Thousands of mysterious containers lie scattered across northern Laos. These “death jars” may have provided a form of communal interment, archaeologists reported.
item.dc:creator: Franz Lidz
